1: yhwh qĕrāʾtîkā ḥûšāh lî hăqîšîbāh qôlî bĕqorʾî-lāk — "O LORD, I call on you — come quickly! Hear my voice when I call to you" — ḥûšāh lî (make haste to/for me, hurry!) — ḥûš (to hasten, make speed) as an urgent imperative — the cry of someone who cannot wait. hăqîšîbāh qôlî (give ear to my voice) — qāšab (to pay attention, listen carefully, prick up the ears) — the prayer is for God's ears to be attuned to this specific voice in this specific moment.
2: tikkôn tĕpillātî qĕṭōret lĕpānêkā maśśĕʾat kappay minḥat-ʿārěb — "may my prayer be set as incense before you, and the lifting of my hands as the evening offering" — qĕṭōret (incense, smoke of burning spices) — the incense altar in the tabernacle/temple burned morning and evening (Exod 30:7-8); the rising smoke visualized prayer ascending to YHWH. maśśĕʾat kappay (the lifting/raising of my palms) — the gesture of open-palmed prayer (1 Kgs 8:22, 38). minḥat-ʿārěb (the evening meal-offering) — the temple's evening grain offering; the psalmist maps his personal prayer to both the incense offering and the grain offering, making prayerless temple worship impossible (the sacrifice without the prayer is incomplete) and temple-less prayer legitimate (the prayer maps to the sacrifice).
3: šîtāh yhwh šomrāh lĕpî nĕṣōr ʿal-delet śĕpātāy — "post a guard at my mouth, O LORD; station a sentry at the door of my lips" — the military metaphors of guarding (šāmar) and the door-sentry (delet) applied to the lips. The gate/door image frames the lips as the entrance to a fortified space: what passes through this gate must be controlled. The prayer is remarkable — the psalmist does not trust his own self-control; he asks YHWH to be the external guard of his speech. nāṣar (to guard, watch over, preserve — used of guarding Torah, secrets, and cities) — a guard of the highest order.
4: ʾal-taṭ libbî lĕdābar rāʿ lĕhitʿôlēl ʿălilôt bĕrešaʿ ʾet-ʾanšê pōʿălê ʾāwen wĕbal-ʾelḥam bĕmanʿammêhem — "do not let my heart be drawn to anything evil — to take part in the wicked acts of evildoers, or to taste any of their pleasures" — ʾal-taṭ libbî (do not incline my heart) — nāṭāh (to extend, bend, incline — used for pitching a tent, stretching out a hand, inclining the ear). The heart can be "bent" toward evil; the psalmist prays for its trajectory to be controlled. ʾelḥam bĕmanʿammêhem (let me not eat their delicacies/pleasures) — manʿammîm (delicacies, pleasant things) — the temptation of complicity is framed as food: tasting the pleasures that come with wickedness.
5: yĕhalmēnî ṣaddîq ḥesed wĕyôkîḥēnî šemen rōʾš ʾal-yānî rōʾšî — "let a righteous man strike me — it is a kindness; let him rebuke me — it is like oil on my head; let my head not refuse it" — yĕhalmēnî (let him strike me, beat me, Qal jussive of hālam) — the same verb for hammer-strikes (Judg 5:26). But the striker is ṣaddîq (righteous) and the striking is ḥesed (covenant love) — blows from a righteous friend are acts of love. šemen rōʾš (oil of the head) — anointing oil as welcome enrichment; the righteous man's rebuke is as enriching as anointing. The counterintuitive theology: honest rebuke from the righteous is to be welcomed more than the flattery of the wicked. James 5:19-20 and Prov 27:6 stand in this tradition.
6: nišmĕṭû bîdê-selaʿ šōpĕṭêhem wĕšāmĕʿû ʾămārāy kî nāʿēmû — "when their leaders are hurled down from the cliffs, the people will find my words welcome" — nišmĕṭû bîdê-selaʿ (they are thrown from the rock/cliff, Niphal of šāmaṭ, to release, let fall, drop) — the violent end of the wicked judges. šōpĕṭêhem (their judges/leaders) — the ones who currently threaten the psalmist. wĕšāmĕʿû ʾămārāy kî nāʿēmû (then they will hear my words, for they are pleasant) — the people who ignored the psalmist's words under oppression will find them welcome when the oppressors fall.
7: kĕmô pōlēaḥ ûbōqēaʿ bāʾāreṣ niprĕdû ʿăṣāmênû lĕpî šĕʾôl — "like clods of earth that are broken up and scattered, our bones lie scattered at the mouth of Sheol" — the difficult verse — the image of plowing/splitting the ground is compared to the scattered bones of the psalmist's companions. pōlēaḥ ûbōqēaʿ (splitting and cleaving) describes the plow's action on hard soil. ʿăṣāmênû (our bones) — the group has suffered such devastation that their bones are already scattered at Sheol's mouth. This may be the extremity of David's exile community rather than literal death.
8: kî ʾēlêkā yhwh ʾădōnāy ʿênāy bĕkā ḥāsîtî ʾal-tĕʿar napšî — "but my eyes are fixed on you, O Lord GOD — in you I shelter; do not leave my soul exposed" — the pivot from the extremity of v7 to fixed gaze. ʿênāy (my eyes) as the directional commitment — the gaze determines the trust. bĕkā ḥāsîtî (in you I have taken refuge) — ḥāsāh (to take refuge, shelter, flee to for protection) — the classic Psalter verb for running to YHWH as one's safe place. ʾal-tĕʿar napšî (do not pour out/expose my soul) — ʿārāh (to lay bare, uncover, pour out) — the soul left exposed like something stripped of its protective covering.
9: šomrēnî mîdê pah yāqĕšû-lî ûmôqĕšôt pōʿălê ʾāwen — "keep me from the trap they have set for me, and from the snares of evildoers" — pah yāqĕšû-lî (the trap they have laid for me) — yāqaš (to lay a bird-snare) — the fowler-imagery connects back to Ps 124:7 and 140:5. The path of the righteous is consistently described in these psalms as surrounded by hidden traps — requiring divine navigation to walk safely.
10: yippĕlû bĕmakmōrāyw rĕšāʿîm yaḥad ʾānōkî ʿad-ʾeʿĕbōr — "let the wicked fall into their own nets, while I pass over safely" — makmōrāyw (his own nets, from kāmar, to ensnare) — the nets the wicked set for others become their own trap. yaḥad ʾānōkî ʿad-ʾeʿĕbōr (while I alone pass over safely) — ʿābar (to cross over, pass through, pass by) — the Passover resonance is unmistakable: the righteous "passes over" while judgment falls on others. The psalm ends with a vision of escape through the very traps the enemy set.
